1: The oracular formula introduces Ezekiel 35, an oracle against Mount Seir (= Edom). Its placement between the shepherd oracle of Ezekiel 34 and the restoration oracle for Israel’s mountains in Ezekiel 36 is structurally significant: Edom’s desolation and Israel’s restoration are the two sides of the same divine act. Ch. 35’s desolation of Mount Seir (Ezekiel 35:3, 4, 7, 9) provides the dark foil for Ezekiel 36’s promise to Israel’s mountains.
2: “Son of man, set your face toward Mount Seir and prophesy against it (hinnavei alav).” Mount Seir (the mountainous region of Edom, southeast of the Dead Sea, modern Jordan) is identified in Genesis with Esau and his descendants (Genesis 36:8: “Esau settled in the hill country of Seir; Esau is Edom”). The Jacob-Esau conflict that began in the womb (Genesis 25:22-23) and continued through the sale of the birthright (Genesis 25:29-34) and the stolen blessing (Gen 27) is the ancestral backstory for the prophetic enmity between Israel and Edom. The directional command “set your face toward” is Ezekiel’s standard prophetic-confrontation posture (Ezekiel 6:2; Ezekiel 13:17; Ezekiel 25:2).
3: “Behold, I am against you, Mount Seir (hineni eleicha har seir).” The adversative formula hineni el (I am against you) that Ezekiel uses for the most severe divine declarations of hostility (against the false prophets, Ezekiel 13:8; against the nations, Ezekiel 26:3; Ezekiel 28:22; Ezekiel 29:3). The promise to “stretch out my hand against you (venatateti yadei aleicha)” uses the standard ANE gesture of divine power-in-action (the “outstretched arm” of divine agency, cf. Exodus 3:20; Ezekiel 6:6; Deuteronomy 4:34). “A desolation and a waste (shemmah umeshammah)” — the identical double formula used for the land of Israel in Ezekiel 33:28, now directed at Edom.
4: “Your cities I will lay waste (arev arev), and you shall become a desolation.” The judgment of Edom’s cities fulfills the earlier oracle against Edom in Ezekiel 25:12-14 and the entire book of Obadiah. The recognition formula “you shall know that I am the LORD” is used here for an enemy nation: YHWH’s identity is disclosed to Edom through judgment, not salvation. The same recognition formula that in Ezekiel 33-37 prompts Israel’s restoration produces a different recognition in Edom: acknowledgment of YHWH’s power over their destruction.
5: “Because you cherished perpetual enmity (eivat olam) and gave over the people of Israel to the power of the sword (higarta livnei Yisrael al-yadei charev).” Eivat olam (perpetual enmity, literally “enmity of eternity/long-standing enmity”) is the causal charge. The Edom-Israel hostility had deep historical roots in the Exodus period: Edom refused Israel passage through their territory (Numbers 20:14-21) and Saul’s wars with Edom (1 Samuel 14:47). “At the time of their calamity, at the time of their final punishment (beet eidam beet avon kets)” — the specific accusation: Edom exploited Israel’s moment of greatest weakness (the 586 BCE Babylonian conquest) to join in the slaughter. Obadiah 10-14 is the most detailed account of Edom’s specific crimes during Jerusalem’s fall: standing aloof, rejoicing, looting, cutting off fugitives.
6: “As I live, declares the Lord GOD, I will destine you for blood (ledamim essa-cha), and blood shall pursue you (vedamim yirdephucha).” The talion principle in covenantal form: Edom handed Israel over to the sword; YHWH will make Edom the target of blood-guilt. “Since you did not hate bloodshed (vedamim lo sane-ta), bloodshed shall pursue you.” The moral logic of covenantal retribution: what a nation welcomes as a tool against others, they receive in return. The phrase “bloodshed shall pursue you” uses the image of blood-guilt as an active pursuer — blood-guilt was in ancient Israelite legal thought a kind of supernatural force that demanded satisfaction (Genesis 4:10: “your brother’s blood cries to me from the ground”).
7: “I will make Mount Seir a waste and a desolation, and I will cut off from it all who come and go (veechrat mimenu over vashaV).” The phrase “cut off all who come and go” describes the complete end of normal human habitation and travel — the paths and routes through Edom’s territory will be abandoned. Edom’s strategic position astride the King’s Highway (the major north-south trade route along the Transjordanian plateau) made it a commercially important territory; cutting off traffic through it removes both the people and their economic function.
8: “I will fill its mountains with the slain (uchaleti et-hareicha chalalim).” The use of harim (mountains) for Edom’s terrain specifically anticipates the contrast with the “mountains of Israel” in Ezekiel 36 — Edom’s mountains will be filled with the slain while Israel’s mountains will be filled with the returned people. “On your hills and in your valleys and in all your ravines those slain by the sword shall fall” — the comprehensive geographic formula (“hills, valleys, ravines”) emphasizes that no terrain feature of Edom escapes. The same geographic comprehensiveness applied to Israel’s restoration in Ezekiel 36:4 (“to the mountains and hills, the ravines and valleys”).
9: “I will make you a perpetual desolation (shemamot olam), and your cities shall not return to be inhabited.” Shemamot olam (perpetual/eternal desolation) is the strongest possible desolation language — it contrasts with Edom’s hope of possessing Israel’s land permanently (Ezekiel 35:10). The historical record: the Nabataean Arab kingdom that occupied Edom’s territory from the 4th century BCE effectively displaced the Edomite population. By the NT period, Idumea (the Hellenistic name for Edom) had been largely absorbed into Judea under John Hyrcanus (125 BCE), and the Edomite population as a distinct entity disappeared. The desolation oracle found its historical fulfillment in stages. The recognition formula closes the verse.
10: “Because you said, ‘These two nations and these two countries shall be mine, and we will possess them’ — although the LORD was there (va-YHWH sham hayah).” The two nations and two countries are Israel and Judah (or the northern and southern kingdoms). Edom’s territorial ambition at the moment of Israel’s collapse (586 BCE) is the second charge, supplementing the blood-guilt of Ezekiel 35:5. “Although the LORD was there” is the pregnant parenthetical that undermines Edom’s possession claim: Edom apparently concluded that YHWH had abandoned the land when Israel was defeated. But Ezekiel consistently insists that YHWH’s departure from the temple (Ezekiel 10-11) was a temporary covenant judgment, not a permanent abandonment. YHWH’s presence in the land makes the land untransferable to another people.
11: “As I live, declares the Lord GOD, I will deal with you according to the anger and envy that you showed through your hatred of them.” The lex talionis applied at the emotional-motivational level: Edom’s anger and envy against Israel become the measure of YHWH’s response against Edom. “I will make myself known among them when I judge you (venodati vahem beshofteki otach)” — YHWH’s self-revelation through the judgment of Edom is for the benefit of Israel: Israel will see and know YHWH through his vindication of them against their ancient enemy. Divine judgment of enemies is, in the covenantal framework, simultaneously defense of the victim.
12: “And you shall know that I am the LORD. I have heard all the insults (neatzu-teicha) you hurled against the mountains of Israel, saying, ‘They are laid desolate; they are given to us to devour.’” The “mountains of Israel” that Edom gloated over in desolation are the very mountains that will be addressed in YHWH’s restoration oracle of Ezekiel 36. Edom’s speech against the mountains of Israel (“they are ours to devour”) is the direct foil for YHWH’s speech to the mountains of Israel (“I will restore you”, Ezekiel 36:1-15). The insults (neatzu) have been heard — prophetic oracles against Edom appear in Isa 34; Jeremiah 49:7-22; Amos 1:11-12; Obadiah; Malachi 1:2-5.
13: “And you spoke arrogantly against me with your mouth and multiplied your words against me; I heard it.” Edom’s insults against Israel’s mountains (Ezekiel 35:12) are simultaneously insults against YHWH: since YHWH is identified with the land and people of Israel, contempt for Israel’s desolation is contempt for YHWH’s apparent defeat. The phrase “multiplied your words against me (higgadetm alay pi-chem)” escalates from insults to arrogant boasting against the divine sovereign. “I heard it (ani shamati)” — the divine hearing of defiant speech is consistently the trigger for prophetic judgment in the OT (Psalms 94:7-11; Isaiah 37:28-29; Ezekiel 25:3, 8, 12).
14: “Thus says the Lord GOD: While the whole earth rejoices (besimchath kol-ha-aretz), I will make you a desolation.” The cosmic reversal: at the moment of Edom’s desolation, the entire earth celebrates. The phrase “the whole earth rejoices” anticipates the restoration of Israel and the nations’ response to it (cf. Psalms 96:11-12; Isaiah 44:23; Ezekiel 49:13). Edom’s desolation is not a private event but a cosmic moment in which YHWH’s justice is publicly displayed. The contrast with Edom’s rejoicing over Israel’s desolation (Ezekiel 35:15) completes the reversal: what Edom did to Israel, YHWH does to Edom, while all the earth watches.
15: “As you rejoiced over the inheritance of the house of Israel because it was desolate, so I will deal with you. You shall be desolate, Mount Seir, and all Edom, all of it.” The precise lex talionis closure: the proportionality of Edom’s desolation to their rejoicing over Israel’s desolation. “Then they shall know that I am the LORD.” The recognition formula closes the oracle as it has closed the other judgment oracles throughout Ezekiel. In the structure of Ezekiel 35-36, Edom learns YHWH’s identity through their judgment (Ezekiel 35:15), while Israel learns YHWH’s identity through their restoration (Ezekiel 36:11) — two modes of the same divine self-disclosure that constitutes the theological purpose of the entire historical movement.
